CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The demonstration at Enniscorthy last Sunday was a
great tribute to brave men who fought. The men of
Wexford who fought in '98 were Pikemen, and Pikemen
are men to respect. The difference between the Wexford
fighting men of ’98 and certain other Irishmen is that the
former did not brag about what they were going to do
by way of doubling up England, they did fight and they
attacked the enemy with real pikes. The Earl of Ports-
mouth would not allow the memorial to be placed on
Vinegar Hill, so it is to be crected in the hoart of the

~ town of Enniscorthy—a far better place from many
oints of view. The memorial will represent the patriot
priest, Father John Murphy leading the way to a young
beasant.  Father Murphy was captured at last ab
Vinegar Hill, and having been whipped and half hanged
was eventually plunged into a barrel of boiling pitch by

e Ohristian soldiers of civilized England that.was
hen and is now so anxious for the salvation of our

“Idolatrous *’ souls. There are “friends’’ of Ireland
cven at the present day who would not turn a hair if
a few hundred Father John Murphy’s were plunged
into barrels of boiling pitch. Father Murphy and his
fighting heroes fought with different weapons than those
that present circumstances call for; the lineal political
descendants of those who plunged him into boiling
pitch have also conformed their tactics to the needs of
the present day.

It would not have been a modern Irish demonstration
if a few touches of raimeis did not adorn it. Our
friends the green Foresters were there, and we read with
a mixed feeling of sadness and amusement that “a large
number of the members wore the Robert Emmet cos-
tume and carried imitation pikes.”” Father Murphy
and his peasant soldiers were not playing at pikery; the
pikes of ‘98 did not stand for rasmess, but for life or
death. Those who carried “imitation pikes’’ might
have shown a nicer sense of what was due to the shades
of the men who fell fighting at Ross and Vinegar Hill.
As Father Fitzhenry in his brief and sensible speech,
said :—“ The lesson he would like them to take away
from that meeting was to be intelligent, self-respecting
Irishmen, knowing and studying the needs of their
motherland, and carrying them out to perfection and
full satisfaction.””

Lord Oranmore and Browne, who is an “ Irishman ’’
by the name of Geoffrey Henry Browne Browne-Guthrie,
and was one time a lieutenant in the 4th Batt. Royal
Scotts Fusiliers, is a comical individual. This noble
gentleman, in the course of an oration on the floor of
the British House of Lords, said :— Owing to religious
differences, very little history could be taught, and what
little was taught appeared to him to consist principally
of a collection of fairy tales, of doughty deeds written
by Erse robber chiefs, who were dignified by the names
of Kings, and who, one by one, succumbed to the ad-
vance of the hated Saxon.”” If the Kings of ancient
Ireland were ““ Erse robber chiefs,”’ with what adjec-
tives should one properly qualify the owners of confis-
cated lands?

Geoffrey Henry Browne Browne-Guthrie, Baron of
Oranmore and Browne, suggested that ¢ Perhaps it
might be made compulsory that the National Anthem
should be taught and sung in every National School.”
The Government should send this ex-lieutenant of the
4th Batt. Royal Scotch Fusiliers to Ireland with orders
to carry out his brilliant suggestions. We picture the
noble baron, with a cane in one hand and a tuning-fork
in the other, teaching a school full of County Cork or
County Mayo children the tune of “send us vict-o-
rious ’’ on the tonic sol-fa system.

The course of the Irish Revival movement has been
marked by the unexpected dropping out of the ranks of
many whom the country could ill spare. To-day the
TIrish movement is the poorer by the untimely death of
Father Michael Moloney, at the early age of 39. The
Irish public had not heard that he had been ailing, and
o the news of his death came as a shock to all who, like
us, knew him and appreciated his excellent qualities and
his earnest work. He was an out-and-out Irish Ire
lander, and did more than one man’s work in variou
ways for the Irish movement. Ie was an aubthority
concerning Church music. He was not a prolific wrltin%
man; he proferred lecturing; and though we recal
soveral kindly private letters to us, the only article from
his pen that appeared in the LeApEr, if our memory
sorves us well, was an article “ The Dublin Lectures on
Church Music”’ which appeared in our issue of July

9th last. In Bermondsey, London, where he laboured

for a'pgfzifgjl of years, ‘he left remembrances of a vast

amount of 'kindly and patriotic work. His connection
with the St. Patrick’s religious services at Bexmondsey,

- and with the great celebration at the Westminster
Cathedral—of which he'was a Canon—this year mani-
fested his.consuming love for faith and fatherland. The
last time we met him was during the Summer of 1904
whilst he was on a holiday in Ireland. He casually
mentioned then that his health was not quite robust;
‘but little would anyone suspect that in less than a full
year thousands of his countrymen who prized him for
his work -and loved him for his kindly, earnest nature,
would have to mourn his untimely loss. May Heaven
be his bed.

The libeller of Canon M‘Inerney has moral scruples
rning “ the King ** incident at the Teachers’ Con-
at Sligo. In a leading article it said:— Every
Christian communion inculcates a respect upon the
part of the youth for those who have been placed over
him by Providence. The occupant of the Throne is far
removed from politics and from party, and it is most
unseemly that he should be made the object of a demon-
stration of ill-will against the Government or the estab-
lished order of things. Teachers, of all men, should
refrain from setting an evil example.”’” Of course, this
is all ranting hypocrisy. The imported editor of the
libeller of Canon M‘Inerney knows as well as we do that
“the King ”’ is, to a great extent, a party toast in Ire-
land notwithstanding that the Dublin members of the
British Institute of Journalists, including many of the
staff of the Freeman, have agreed that it should be
toasted at their dinners; “ God Save the King ’’ means,
in most cases in this country, “ Damn the ‘ Idolators’
and Nationalists.””

Of course, it is very hard to fight the fallacies of the
Irish Times when we find the Freeman wobbling like
a lump of jelly.  The Institute of Journalists toast
“the King "’ at their dinner, and several members of
the staff of the “truly National ”’ Freeman are mem-
bers of that body of ““the King’’ compact. Yet the
Freeman of Friday last wrote:—‘In Ireland Orange-
men claim a -monopoly of loyalty. The toast of the
King’s health has been made a party test, and ‘God
Save the King’ a party tune in Ireland. They are
both employed as an offensive challenge to Nationalists
to renounce their Nationality.”” So, according to the
Freeman, “ the King *’ is made a party test, and “ God
Save the King ’’ is a party tune. That is our view pre-
cisely ; but what of the Inmstitute of Journalists and
Messrs. Brayden, Donovan, etc., of the Freeman?

The libeller of Canon M‘Inerney is anxious that the
national schoolmasters who refused to rise to “the
King *’ at Sligo should be punished. The Bigots’ Dust
Din says:—“ One would have thought that the autho-
rities would have lost no time in marking their sense
of the behaviour of men who, though paid by the State,
do not hesitate to insult the head of the State.”” Now
national public opinion is in a chronic state of muddle
as to the attitude which Nationalist Ireland should
We tackled the question
straightforwardly on several occasions, but Nationalist
Ireland prefers to remain in a state of muddle. We have
the Freeman declaring with us that “the King’’ is a
party toast, yet its Mr. Brayden and its Mr. Donovan
are of the Institute that has made a compact that “ the
King *’ is to be toasted.

Now Mr. Braydén and Mr. Donovan, and others like
them, appear to demonstrate that the anti-King atti-
tude is untenable in practice; it is yet quite true that
“the King ” in the present circumstances is a party
toast. The situation is entangled, but it would
straighten itself out before a vigorous and honest effort,
toface it. 'We have attempted before now to apply
logic to the situation, but our example was not followed
by what we may call the orthodox Nationalists.  All
we wish to point out at the moment is that the Free-
man ls:ggn an utterly ri(ijc\lx}9 I;'a;jd som ﬁt of a con-

temptible position. If its Mr. Brayden and its M,

“Donovan and the rest are members of an Institute with

a “XKing ”’ compact, how ridicu}ous it appears marching
in a literary torchlight procession round the anti-King
Teachers of the Sligo €ongress! If it is too much to
ask the Freeman to be straight, might we suggest that
it should make some attempt to avoid being utterly
ridiculous.

Who compose the Dublin Metropolitan National
Teachers’ Association? It is reported in the Press that
“at a largely-attended meeting *’ the following resolu-
tion was unanimously adopted :—* That we condemn
in the strongest manner the disloyalty exhibited by. a
large number of those who attended the Congress din-
ner in Sligo; that we regard the conduct of these men
as despicable and a disgrace to an organisation cgmposed
of State-paid teachers, and that their action is calcu-
lated to alienate from the teachers’ cause a powerful
and influential section of the Irish public; that we call
upon the Executive to repudiate on the first opportunity
and in the name of the Organisation, the action of those
men, who have brought such a stigma on the National
teachers of Ireland, and that should the Executive fail
to do this we shall have to consider our relation with
regard to the general organisation.”” This is pretty
“hot;”” those who object to “the King’’ as a party
toast in this country are declared guilty of “ despicable’
conduct. The Freeman agrees with us that as things
go “ the King " is a party toast. Yet by formal com-
pact “ the King ’’ is toasted at the dinner of the Insti-
tute of Journalists.

In what state are the political Nationalists of this
country at present in relation to the reigning Monarch
in these realms? Are they rebels, loyalists, tin-rebels,
lip-loyalists, all things by turns as occasion calls, or
what? We know very well what state they are in, and
it is a state they do not show any disposition to emerge
out of—they are in a state of absolute muddleheaded-
ness. A little straight thinking is badly needed. Even
our ex-Tin Pikers are “ God Save the King’s Most Ex-
cellent Majesty, the Lords and Commons of Ireland ’’
men now !

The second annual feir Cille-Oapa  will be held
in the old Gaol Grounds, Naas, on Sunday and Monday,
18th and 19th inst. Kildare is following the lines of
organization on which County Wexford works, and the
County Committee, formed two years ago, by a few
workers scattered through the County and comprising
at its start only five branches, has grown to such dimen-
sions that it was found necessary last month to sub-
divide it into three District Committees, each under the
care of one of the Coisde’s organizing teachers and
governed by the central body. Rach of these organi-
zers teaches on an average seven branches per week
besides attending to the schools in his district. Wex-
ford has proved the efficacy of such a scheme of organi-
zation, and Kildare through its adoption is rapidly in-
creasing the influence of the League in the County.
We understand that even yet Irish is hardly considered
“class” in Naas. Although the Coipoe provides ex-
tern teachers free for the schools in its area in which
Irish could not otherwise be ‘taught, the Chuistian
Brothers’ was the only school within a radius of five
mxle:s from Naas which presented competitors at last
year's Teip. We believe Naas is chosen as the venue
from its being the most central town in the County; we
hope it will prove more worthy this year from an Irish
Ireland point of view of the great annual festival. The
Commx.tt.ee are organizing in connection with the feip
an exhibition of local industries.

We learn that at a recent meeting of the Committee
of the Munster Feis, 1905, it was proposed to hold a
Banquet in connection with the Munster Conference
next August in Killarney. This Conference, as has
been frequently stated in these columns, will be held
on Monday the 7th August, 1905, and the Feis Com-
mittee propose to make arrangements for a banquet
in the evening of this day if the delegates appointed ex-
press a general desire to participate in it. We have
therefore been asked by the Committee to remind those
who intend to be present at this banquet to communi-
cate at once with the Rev. C. Brennan, Killarney.
Further information will be supplied in the circular
about to be issued to the Gaelic League Branches of
Munster, and it is sufficient to mention here that all the
toasts to be proposed and the speeches given ia reply
must be in Irish.

‘Whatever may be said Moore
as a poet, his position as a popular Anglo-Irish
lyrist is beyond assault. And if the Irish of these times

f% have of necessity to sing songs in English, how poorly

concerning Tom

the made-in-England lyrics one is accustomed to at
concerts compare with such songs as “ Rich and Rare,”
and ‘“She is far from the Land.”’” Moore’s melodies
had a field night at the memorial concert on last Mon-
- - day, when a large audience crowded every part of the
} round room in the Mansion House. ~Mr. Joseph
‘ O’Mara’s singing of “ When he who adores thee’’ was

worth going a journey to hear, and the other items on
the programme including an Irish version by Dr.
McHale of “ The Last Rose of Summer,’” were also much

Some interesting camoguitea¢t was played at Rich-
mond Hill, on Sunday last. In the first match the
Keating Branch beat Rathmines Gaelic League. The
pupils of Muckross convent who have acquired much
skill at the game then played Dundrum Gaelic League
and scored an easy victory. i

“ Remember ‘98’ largely means “ Remember Wex-
ford,” and in the movement towards an Irish Ireland
modern Wexford is not lagging behind, as we hope the
forthcoming feip Lo¢ Fapmamn will further show.
We are glad to hear that over 3,000 entries have been
received for this Féis, which will take place in New Ross,
on Whit-Sunday and Monday, 11th and 12th of June.

p Special trains at cheap fares will run on both days from

gaged for the occasion. The industrial exhibition .in
connection with the Feis promises to be very successful
and will continue for three days.

Who is Mr. T.'J. O’Kelly of the Custom House,
Cork? Is he a cycle expert? A local Cork dealer, in a
certain make of British bicycle, issued a booklet of testi-
monials concerning the British cycle which he’ vends.
Mr. T. J. O’Kelly of the Custom House, Cork, in the
course of his letter, as published in the advertising boqk-
let, says:—I must say my has given me entire
satisfaction in every respect, and I heartily wish some of
our Irish firms of cycle manufacturers would turn out
an equally good machine.”” We assume that Mr. T. J.
O’Kelly of the Custom House, Cork, must be an expert,
or else-he would scarcely give this gratuitous back-
hander to Irish bicycle-makers in his puff of a British
machine. How many Irish bicycles has he tested with a
view of comparing their respective values with the value
of the British make he puffed ? By what laborious method
has this gentleman arrived at the conclusions that
“some of our Irish firms ”’ cannot turn out a bicycle to
compete with a certain British make?

The blaze of trumpets with which certain long-felt
wants ’’ are brought into this world is in great contrast
with the dread silence in which they prematurely
depart from this life. Readers, prepare to shed
your tears—Dana is dead! In our issue of
7th May, 1904, we said “ A production by the name of
Dana is the latest ‘ long-felt want ’ to make its appear-
ance.”” Dana was ‘“a magazine. of independent
thought "’—as our low-down readers know, the LEADER
is only a base “ proxy thinker!” We may recall that
in the world-moving introduction which hurled. Dana—
which is an Irish word signifying brave, intrepid—into
this world, there was an invitation to “the thinkers,
dreamers, and observers dispersed throughout Ireland
and elsewhere, who do not despair of humanity in Ire-
land, to communicate through our pages their thoughts,
reveries, and observations.””” The dreamers, “thinkers’’
—thinkers, indeed—observers, and non-despairers ral-
lied to Dana. Even an article from Sir Horace himself
graced its pages; the unconsciously comical novelist now
happily “ making his soul °’ told the world through the
first number of Dana that he could not tell anyone why
he loyed a canal, and yet the spots remained on the
sun, and the world continued to go round. We said of
the first number—* It is published in Dublin and Lon-
don, and is a questionable pennyworth offered for sale
at sixpence.”’ When will the world dry its tears, for,
alas and alack! Dana—Dana is now dead. The Bigots’
Dust Bin took Dana under its patronage and puffed it—
yet Dana of the intellectuals is dead! Dana of the
spooks has vanished in the night, and “independent
thought *” is left lonely and desolate weeping on the
lonely edges of the Grand Canal. The “sober Nation-
alist’” must be inconsolable now that the doughty cham-
pion of the dreamers and non-despairers is stiff and
stark, and those possessed of the demon of “ hatred of
thought or incapacity to think *’ no more need tremble
at the war-snorting of its mighty nostrils. Drape the
mountains in mournful mist, and let the reeds by the
lake edges send forth the melancholy caoine on the
four winds of Erin—the mighty one has fallen, the
hope of Ireland is once more blasted, Dana—Dana is
dead !

The Bazaar at Lusk begins to-day (Thursday), and
will be continued on the 3rd and 4th inst. Amongst
the attractions are camozurocacéz, choirs, bands, ete.
The return fare from Dublin is 1s., and special trains
will run until mid-night.

There is a considerable amount of dissatisfaction with
the Mandeville Memorial Committee on account of their
having given the contract for the erection of the memo-
rial to a British irm. The voting as between a local con-
tractor and a Dublin one was six a side; and the Com-
mittee eventually got out of the difficulty by unani-
mously sending the work to Britain! It appears that

T R S S

fones, of Hartlepool, who got the contract, is a

- FEa]
~ member of the United Irish League of Great Britain;

hat fact, however, is scarcely adequate compensation
to this country for the loss of the contract.

" At a meeting of the Mitchelstown Branch of the Gae-

lic League several resolutions were unanimously passed
with reference to the matter. The branch condemus ‘‘as
unpatriotic and unnational the action of the Mandeville
Memorial Committee in refusing the tenders of Irish-
men and accepting unanimously that of an English
firm.  We cannot help regarding their conduct as in a
certain sense a betrayal of their trust towards the sub-
scribers, who certainly would never consent to have
Irish talent so ignored and Irish monics so misapplied,
especially at a time when there is a general movement
to revive native industries. What aggravates the case
is the fact that these men acted against the expressed
views of Mr. William O’Brien, M.P., and even accepted
the highest tender. It is no wonder that the Press was
excluded from their meeting.”’  The branch also calls
“on the County and District Councils to refuse a site
for this monument until the Committee employ native
talent in the execution of the work.”” Perhaps the Com-
wittee would now explain to the public their reasons

. for sending the work out of the country.

The Feis Ceotl was the outstanding event of last week
in the Irish world. The mere news about the various
contests has already been placed in the possession of the
public by the various daily papers; it only remains for
us to present to our readers a general appreciation or
criticism of this year’s function. We had hoped to do
this in the present issue, but we must ask our readers
to be patient until next week. We regret that there
is a delay, but as the Feis Ceoil only ended on Saturday
last, we feel sure that they will not mind our asking
them to wait a week for a good thing.

In consequence of the stimulus of the Industrial Re-
vival the market for Irish goods in Britain has been in
several directions largely expanded. The expansion is,
of course, only comparative, and a vast profitable field
for the sale of Irish products remains untouched. Our
Irish readers in England have done a lot of pioneer werk
for Irish Industry there, and, happily, several firms
trading in England have thrown themselves heartily
into the movement for opening up British markets for
TIrish goods. Amongst these firms are Messrs. Corrigan
and French of Roseberry Avenue, Falcon Avenue, and
Aldersgate Street, London. We have received from
this firm a neat booklet entitled, “ To Stem the Tide of
Emigration.””  They are pushing the sale of Irish
tweeds and homespuns, Irish caps and hats, poplin ties,
Irish umbrellas, braces and hosiery. We read in the
booklet :—“ It is in the power of the Irish in Great
Britain to provide that employment by purchasing
Irish-made goods. It may be safely said that in London
alone the Irish population, by adopting this course,
could keep hundreds of villages and small towns in Ire-
land steadily and lucratively employed all the year
through.”” ~ And again:—“ Unfortunately the native
article suffers in reputation through the pernicious prac-
tices of unscrupulous dealers, who substitute foreign

On Whit Sunaay, 11th Jume, 190.

After the First Gospel of the Last Mass,

A CHARITY SERMON

Will ached in
ST. MICHAEL’S CHURCH, KINGSTOWN,
By the Rev. ROBERT KANE, S.J.,

IN AID OF
St. Michael’s Hospital
(Under the care of the Sisters of Mercy),
KINGSTOWN.

The Sisters of Mercy confidently appeal to the pro-
verbial liberality of the Benefactors of the Suffering
Poor to give them such help as will enable them to con-
tinue this great work of mercy.

The Sisters avail of this opportunity to express their
heartfelt gratitude to all who have so perseveringly and
cordially supported St. Michael’s Hospital since its
opening.

The attractive promise of the Great Healer—“ You
did it unto Me,’” ought to be a magnet to draw forth
help for the Sick from every benevolent heart; then
“As long as you did it to one of these my least brethren’”
‘Where shall we find them? In hospital, stricken down
by disease, weary, weak, dependent, truly they are
“least brethren "’ whom the Divine Rewarder wishes to

personate, and will you then, who are blessed with

riches, aid to maintain them, to pillow their aching
heads, to reach the refreshing cup to their parched lips,
and to relieve their agonising pain surgically or medi-
cally.

Donations will be thankfully received by His Grace
the Most Rev. Dr. Walsh, Archbishop of Dublin; by
the Very Rev. W. J. Canon Murphy, P.P.; by the
Parochial Clergy of Kingstown and the adjoining
parishes; by the Sisters of Mercy, Baggot Street, Dub-
lin; and at St. Michael’s Hospital, Kingstown, Ireland.

A REVIEW OF EDUCATION IN IRELAND.—IIL

Before the confiscation of Catholic property the schools
of this country were supported out of means which eccle-
siastical authorities held in trust for these and other pur-
poses. The legalised spoiler came, and the people and
their Church were left beggars and outlaws in their own
land ; left without a school in which to instruct their
children, or a house in which to adore their God. Out
of that confiscated property, besides Parliamentary
Grants from the Treasury, were founded and maintained
Trinity College, the Parish Schools, the Diocesan Free
Schools, the Royal Free Schools, the Charter Schools,
the Hibernian Military and Marine Schools, the Kildare
Btreet Society Schools, and other Endowed Schools down
to our own day, the acknowledged purpose of some of
them, and the real object of all, being to Anglicise and
to Protestantise the people. It was the plain purpose
to make them renegades from their nation and traitors
to their faith; and what was above all wicked in the
policy was that they were to pay the cost of their own
perversion. They were deprived of their property, and
then reviled as paupers; they were disabled by an ini-
quity which legislators call “law >’ from teaching or be-
ing taught unless at the cost of their faith and their
national honour, and then held up to scorn for being
illiterate. After millions of acres and of money had
been plundered from, and then used against them ; after
they had been beaten to the ground, had, like the early
Christians in the Catacombs, hidden the mysteries of
their Faith amidst the mountains, and kept their na-
tional spirit alive in the bogs and in the woods, they came
out and appeared again at the opening of the last cen-
tury with the freshness, the hope, and the vigour of
youth. And yet they are without strenuous qualities.
They provided their own primary schools till the Na-
tional system came, and they have, for the most part,
out of their private resources, paid for the schools which
have been built under that system down to this day. Sir
Thomas Wyse * states that the Irish priests provided for
the education of four times as many children “ with in-
finitely less than one-twentieth part of the means’’ than
did the State-endowed Charter Schools. He says that
a priest in Lewisburg, Co. Sligo,  established no less
than thirteen schools with little other assistance than
what he derived from his own exertions,”” and that
“there are many similar instances to be found in many
parts in Ireland.”” Father Tom Maguire, the celebrated
controversialist, established nine schools over his exten-
sive parish amidst the mountains of Leitrim. I have
been told by one who has seen the wills of several priests
of those days that those who had any money saved left
it mostly for the buildings of schools for the people.
Froude writes of the Irish Catholic Schools of the 18th
century :— The Catholics, with the same steady cour-
ago and unremitting zeal with which they had main-
tained and multiplied the number of their priests, had

The clergy, and those of the gentry and nobility who
survived confiscation, were, for the most part, educated
abroad, and were brought home after the manner of all
contraband goods. Oue who lives quite near where I
write" tells' me that her great grand-uncle, who had a
distinguished course in one of the Continental Univer-
sities, was stowed away in a barrel and shipped back to
Ireland. Itis not so long ago; for he built a church in
the town where I write in the year 1799. But the sup-
pression of the Irish Colleges of France and the Low
Countries during the Revolution, and the disturbed state
of the Continent generally towards the close of the 18th
century, shut out Irish students in great measure from
those opportunities. On the other hand, the dawn of
liberty was breaking at home, prepared by the War of
American Independence and heralded by the indepen-
dence of the Volunteers; the first Relief Act was passed

*1n 1793, and the Catholics of Ireland at once set about
making provision for higher education at home. On that
first year of dawning toleration, St. Patiick’s College,
Carlow, was founded; it may therefore be called the
Nestor of our Catholic Colleges. Next came Maynooth
in 1795, specially founded for the education of priests.
The Bishops desired that the lay Catholics should also
share its advantages, and a department for lay students
was opened in 1801. The Maynooth lay College was
closed in 1817; for which the Bishops, of course, are
blamed, and they have been recently abused for that
additional attempt to preclude the laity from enlighten-
ment in order to crush their liberty. Dr. Starkie, hur-
ried away on his hobby of co-ordinated education, thinks
that haying ““no root-fibres in a system of primary and
secondary schools, it soon languished and died.”” His
account of the case is certainly more respectable than
that which is given by those who allege, as one of the
acts of episcopal injustice to the Irish Catholic laity, that
they closed the lay College in Maynooth, and closed on
the funds into the bargain. The truth is that when the
lay College was opened, the Maynooth authorities were
warned by the Government that it would not be sanc-
tioned, inasmuch as it was against the “original pur-
pose of the College.”” Government was opposed to it

country, their liberty, thousands of them their lives—
overything except their God, their faith, and their con-

they were freed from their fetters, and have, out of their
poverty and exclusively with their private resources,
organised a system of Intermediate Education more com-
plete and better, within the span of a century, than the
Government through confiscation, monopoly, and public
endowments has been able to do for its favourites in

The Model Schools were, in their original purpose,
part of a co-ordinating plan. They were meant to be
Intermediate Schools to prepare the way for the Queen’s
Colleges, although they were established in connection
with the National Schools, and are contTolled by the
Commissioners. 5

They cost no less than £500,000 to build and fur-
nish; for more than two generations they have been
carrying on their work at a cost of about £35,000 a
year; at the time of the Powis Commission at any rate
they had a teacher for every ten pupils; they have had
the unspeakable advantage of being quite free from
priestly interference or control; have never been under
the shade of Catholic obscurantism ; have been managed
by a succession of Resident Commissioners, according to
what Dr. Starkie would call the “independent and edu-
cated’’ lay mind ; have been attended by the children of
the well-off middle classes instead of by the children of
the poor for whom they were professedly intended—
‘Well, what have they done? They have educated con-
siderably less than 6,000 pupils in average annual atten-
dance. A Resident Commissioner in 1866 gave as a
reason why they should not be abolished, that “ they
are amongst the best examples in the world of sound
secular instruction.”’ They were glorified in the In-
spectors’ Reports and by the Board’s officials generally.
The people generally, accustomed as they had been,
owing to non-Catholic privilege and Catholic poverty and
exclusion, to expect educational superiority from what
was non-Catholic or secular, believed all they had heard
or read of them. Who could doubt, after the Annual
Reports of Commissioners, the highest official praises,
Parliamentary assurances of excellence, that the Model
Schools were all that they professed to be? or that the
vast amount of public money they had cost was well
and wisely spent? But they hardly rival the official
praises of the old Charter Schools, and yet we know the
revolting exposure which Mr. Howard and a Royal Com-
mission made of these. Similarly, another Royal Com-
mission came—the Powis Commission of 1869%—and

HE one special trait which the various Government

I Boards that practically rule Ireland are all con-
spicuously alike in, is, as I have more than once men-
tioned, their unsympathetic, if not supercilious, treat-
ment of the opinions of the public elected bodies, and the
majority of the people who elect them. If these Boards
would steer an even keel, and not import into the ad-
ministration of the affairs of the various bodies they
supervise and rule, their ultra-loyal, but unnational,
sentiments, or if they, at least, took the broul?le to con-
coal their contempt for the public clected bodies who, as
Lord Dunraven, in his latest speech at Manchester,
said, “in their management of public affairs act with a
marvellous intelligence, a wonderful prudence, and a
sterling honesty and industry,”” I should feel less dis-
posed to severely criticise their too often petty and spite-
ful actions. It is, however, their bias against, their un-
concealed contempt, and their disregard of the feelings
and aspirations of these public bodies, who, unpaid for
their duties, and who, striving to do the best for their
own localities, and for the good of their country gener-
ally, makes one unduly critical when dealing with the
shortcomings and inconsistencies of these Boards who
lord it over the local bodies.

These Boards all take their cue from our English
governors in the House of Parliament. That open con-
tempt, that utter disregard of Irish national opinion,
that unwillingness to grant even the smallest favour or
concession—even in cases where no direct issue is at stake
—that air of we-know-better-than-you-do, that school-
boy sort of treatment with which Irish Chief Secretaries
and Irish law officers at Westminster attempt to treat
Irish members—attempt, I say, for they do not always
succeed—is copied by the public departmental officials
here in Ireland, and is looked on as the just, traditional
way in which to treat public bodies who are the choice
and the representatives of the people and of public
opinion. When we consider how little opportunity Irish-
ien have had in the past to learn the art of local go-
vernment, or to deal with matters of finance, educa-
tion, and technical training, as Lord Dunraven says, it
is really wonderful how men have applied themselves,
and how admirable have been their administration of
public matters and public affairs.”’ Even the worst
cnemies of Ireland have had to bear testimony to their
capable management of county government and local
finance, and their efforts to attain economy without im-
pairing efficiency. Yet the last to recognise these qua-
lities and the first to deny them are these public Boards
who practically run the local government of the country
—viz., the Agricultural Department, the Board of Pub-
lic Works, and the Local Government Board itself.
‘Why is this? It is not far to seek the reason. Every
successful effort of the local bodies is a contrast and an
object lesson to the purblind efforts and failures of these
Government Boards, and they resent it.

I have shown how crooked and narrow and devious are
the ways of the Agricultural Department. How un-
Trish, how un-national it is, whenever the opportunity
offers to listen to, or be guided by, Irish opinion.
How prone it is to despise Irish sentiment, or, indeed,
to recognise Irish ability in any way, and how lax to
foster Irish talent or Irish trade. Take an ex-
ample:—For some inscrutable reason of its own,
now that tobacco culture has, after a weary and
costly trial—for which the Department can scarcely
take much credit—proved to be a success, the
Agricultural Board, instead of giving an opportunity of
spreading the growth and culture of the tobacco plant
over as many counties as possible, seems rather to re-
strict and to confine the industry to two or three centres.

FATHER FINLAY’S UNIVERSITY SCHEME.

Dear Sir,—Would it not be worth the country’s
while to give serious consideration to Father Finlay’s
University proposal? His article in this month’s New
Ireland Review, explains the scheme clearly. It is
simple and practicable, and unless there be some mis-
calculation in it or some flaw in the machinery, or some
objection to be made on other grounds, the undertaking
he advocates seems an easy, useful and even necessary
one for Ireland in her highest interests.

The proposal is that pending the obtaining of a Na-
tional University from Parliament the people of Ire-
land should, without further delay, establish an insti-
tution in Dublin for providing an efficient cowrse of
lectures for the degrees of the Royal University in the
different faculties, with scholarships to be won by com-
petition for about two hundred youths, admitting them
free to the lectures and to maintenance in the houses of
residence.

Of course, this would not settle the University ques-
tion at all, it would not supply us with a National Uni-
versity, nor relieve the Government of the duty of
supplying us, nor weaken our demand on Parliament
for it, nor postpone its granting. On the contrary, it
would immensely assist our agitation for our educational
rights. It would prove our earnestness, our sense of
our necessities, our fidelity to our convictions, and also
the injustice of our present deprivation better than any
profession of them can do.

No Saxon could be so wanting in humour as not to
realise the satire on his Government of the picture sug-
gested by Father Finlay—Trinity College on one side
with its spacious buildings maintained by a grant from
the State of the plundered lands of the Irish race, and
across Stephen’s Green a structure hardly more preten-
tious than a shed of galvanised iron, with the sign-
‘board “ University Institute for Irish Catholics. Sup-
ported by voluntary contributions.’””

But what is of much more consequence than furnish-
ing the most telling argument of our grievances and our
needs, the establishment of this Catholic College would
provide the bare essentials of University education for
most of our talented youths who are at present debarred
from it, and would lessen the terrible waste of brain-

—as greab a loss'as that of all our water-power that is

Such a National University Institute would un-
doubtedly be assisted to open its doors w1dgr by be-
quests, gifts, and the foundation of scholarships by in-

Trishmen of large or moderate means who would not

The Institute would, of course, also benefit from the
fees and pensions of paying pupils who would be attracted
to so important a teaching centre in puml.:vers largely
exceeding the present attendance of University students
at Dublin or other Colleges.

Facilities for preliminary education are also a part
of this scheme. The Intermediate schools of the
country, Father Finlay suggests, should establish a
system of free places for their whole course to be won by
examination by the best pupils of the Primary Schools.
And it is amongst these pupils of the Intermediate
Schools he would have the scholarships for the Uni-
versity College competed for. The parochial subscrip-
tions raised in each diocese would make scholarships for
the students of that diocese only, and thus education
would be provided free through the Primary, Interme-
diate and University stages for those with talent enough
to make it useful to them.

The Intermediate Schools, he points out, could well
afford to give these free places to promising students out
of the substantial result fees they would earn for their
proficiency in the Intermediate examinations. The ini-
tial capital expenses on such a University institution
would not be heavy, and might be met by gifts from
individuals. The University College buildings in St.
Stephen’s Green, would, it is presumed, be available,
but their extension and the acquisition of houses of
residence would have to be provided for.

It would be necessary before proceeding further with
the consideration of this project to learn the views of
our Bishops upon it. If they are satisfied on religious
grounds with the proposed constitution, would it not
be desirable to have a conference or meeting of all in-
terested in the scheme to examine the wisdom or un-

obtaining financial support. 5

Are there half-a-dozen men of influence and judgment

Ireland an opportunity of considering it: who will
seek the opinion of the Bishops, and if that be not un-
favourable, enquire into the willingness of the Inter-
mediate school managers to co-operate in the scheme,
and then invite the General Council and the various
County Councils to put it before the country for accep-
tance or rejection.

to forget that it, too, is a product of the Gaelic Movement, and,
in the fight against Shoncenism, West Britonism and bigotry,
where minced words and gloved hands will not avail, it squares
up in an attitude which, though necessarily there, should be aban-
doned when matters of moment and of mutual interest have to be
discussed among the forces of Irish Ircland: The LEMADER never
intimated that the funds of the Gaelic League were being used
other than to the best advantage, according to the honest judgment
of those directing its policy and operations, but a man of the in-
sight of its Editor must know that his questions and comments
would be misinterpreted by some honest persons, and deliberately
misconstrued by others. Then its tone was rather of a dictatorial
character, and by those unacquainted with the LEADER'S services
would be adjudged unfriendly. It is human nature to resent a
peremptory demand, where a friendly request would be gladly
complied with. The fear of impartial observers and admirers of
the LEADER is whether its tendency in this direction is not lessen-
ing its power to do good. That would be a regrettable result, not
for the LEADER'S sake, but for the sake of the Irish Ireland Move-
ment, in which it has been so conspicuously able a factor. But,
as already intimated, all this has absolutely nothing to do with
the disgraceful treatment accorded to one who has done so much
for that cause, while the editor of the Orange Afail or the bigoted
Times would undoubtedly have been allowed to participate un-
molested if they desired to do so.

THE NATIONAL BOARD OF EDUCATION. 
RULE 127 b MODIFIED. 
W 
E have seen that the National Board, frightened 
out of its position by the storm of indignation 
with which 127 b has been received throughout the 
country, has adopted certain modifications of the Rule. 
'fhese modifications were published, after receiving the 
Treasury sanction, in the daily papers of 25th ult. Aud, 
by the way, if it is true that the Treasury people had 
nothing to do· with the framing of 12·7 b, and that it 
was not intended as a device for saving them money, how 
comes it that their sanction was required for its modi-
fication ? Sm-ely it is not pretended that these people 
take an atom of interest in Irish education, beyond 
seeing that the expense of carrying it on is kept at the 
lowest possible :figm·e. 
As I have said, the modified 
Rule is now public property, and what do the modifica-
tions amount to? Under the original rule a boy was 
not supposed, in the words of a statement drawn up by 
t!te County Kerry Teachers' Association, to have ceased 
to be a. girl until he attained to about the age of eight 
years and a half. 
Well, the Oommissioners have put 
_ on, their considering caps and they now find that he com-
mences to belong to his own sex exactly a year younger. 
What wonderful anthropologists these Commissioners of 
-ours are, to be sure ! I hear there are some provisions 
for the retention of boys of any age in a boys' school 
if " in the opinion of the Commissioners the probable 
effect of their removal will be the loss of an assistant 
teacher to the school." In other words, instead of 
being cleared out unceremoniously, a number of men 
assistants, hitherto secure in their positions will be 
allowed to remain on in sufferance, the " opinion of the 
Commissioners" being the straw which they are to 
hang on by. Now I venture to say that if the opinions of 
the Commissioners have displayed any one characteristic 
more than another in recent years it has been insta-
., bility ; so that I feel justified in asserting that the new 
provisions afford but very poor security for the men 
assistants and that the latter will find they have been 
living in a fool's paradise if they put much trust in 
them. 
I_ find nothing in the amended Rule to safeguard 
the rights of those meri Principals for whom the trans-
ference of the young boys from their schools would 
mean lowering in grade, or deprivation of the rights of 
promotion which they had hitherto enjoyed; nor do I 
see in it an ypromise to indemnify Principals or Assis-
tants for the losses in capitation fees they may suffer. 
These are " unconsidered trifles " in the eyes of the 
Commissioners; but I should like to see the row that 
some of them, who are .safaried servants of the State 
themselves, would kick up if it were proposed to take a 
little clip off their incomes for any cause, no matter how 
much it might be in lin~ with the· public benefi.~, The· 
situation, as Mr. MacHugh pµt it at Sligo, is positively 
maddening. 
The Irish people who have to bear the 
cost of Irish primary education-for be it 1·emembered 
that Ireland pays more than her proportionate share 
towards the Imperial Exchequer and whose children at-
tend the primary schools-do not wish the teachers to be-
ill-treated ; on the contrary they desire that they should 
be well paid and well provided for in every way. .But 
the views or wishes of the Irish people count fo1· abso-
lutely nothing in the eyes of twenty gentlemen in Dub-
lin who repi·esent nobody, who would no more think of 
sending their own children to primary schools than of 
sending them to be brought up in the work~1ouse, many 
of whom I venture to say never saw a Nat10nal Schoof 
except from the outside, and all of whom have shown 
their utter lack of National pride, and confessed their 
utter incapacity as educationists by tamely adopting all 
the views dictated to them by an English Inspector who-
spent about two months .flitting about ainong the schools 
of the country. 
_ 
The general question whether lady teachers are more 
fitted to have the care of young boys than men teachers 
is one which no sensible body of men should ever have 
attempted to settle by a rule. In some cases a lady 
teacher may have a better knack of dealing with the 
young lads than the man teacher of the ad ioining 
school ; in other cases the man teacher may be the more 
competent of the two in this way. The rational course 
would be to let the individual parents decide the matter 
aq far as their own children are concerned, having reaard 
to their knowledge of the teachers, and I daresay that 
t;he Commissioners would have been very well content to 
let that arrangement stand if they had been left to 
themselves ; but Mr. Dale came along with his orders, 
and they meekly accepted them, and threw the whole 
country into an uproar, and some of them assumed the 
role of strong men, and told the teachers they were 
butting their heads against stone walls in opposing the 
rule, and so 011. 
Great is the power of an English In-
spector of Schools truly ! 
A friend of mine in whose 
dialectical skill I have considerable confidence intends 
one of these fine days to start the theory that men tea-
chers are more fitted to have the care of girls under 
eight than women teachers. He doesn't hope to get it 
adopted by the Commissioners at first, but he has some 
expectation of being appointed an Inspector of Schools 
in England, and then he'll be in a position to impose his 
will on the Commissioners of Irish National Education. 
Let the lady assistants then look out. I don't think 
the wbole situation regarding Rule 127 b could be better 
put than it was by the Ven . .Archdeacon O'Leary, of 
Kenmare, in a letter to the recent public meeting at 
Tralee. 
"The rule," he said, " is bad ; it is radically 
bad; and it cannot be satisfactorily modified. "
